# /gdocs-meeting-notes-template

Fill meeting notes template from calendar event.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Five minutes before a meeting (or the night before), the user wants a notes doc ready: pre-filled with date, attendees, and agenda straight from the calendar event, structured with the sections they'll actually fill during the call (decisions, action items). Beats starting from a blank "Untitled document" while people are joining.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Which meeting: today's next event (default), or named event/date
- Template source: an existing notes-template Doc in Drive to copy, or generate the standard structure (Attendees / Agenda / Discussion / Decisions / Action items)
- Where the doc goes: project folder if one matches the meeting, else the user's notes folder
- Recurring meetings: fresh doc per occurrence, or append to a running doc (many teams keep one rolling doc per recurring series)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Workspace-native formatting. Return Doc/Sheet/Slide links.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-drive** - Google Drive
  - MCP server key: `google-drive`
  - Example tools: `google-drive__search_files`, `google-drive__read_file`, `google-drive__create_file`

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Creating the notes doc is a write: show what will be created (name, folder, pre-filled content) and wait for `apply`.
- Never attach the doc to the calendar event or share it with attendees without a separate explicit confirmation - sharing notifies people.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Calendar and Drive tools. Fetch the target event and extract: title, date/time, attendee names (mark organizer; skip declined attendees), the description/agenda text, and any docs already attached to the invite.
2. Check for an existing notes doc first - the organizer may have attached one, or a previous occurrence of a recurring meeting may have a running doc. If found, offer to use/extend it instead of creating a duplicate.
3. Prepare the doc content:
   - Title per convention: `<YYYY-MM-DD> <Meeting title> - notes`.
   - Header: date, attendees (from the invite, not guesses), organizer.
   - Agenda section: pull real agenda items from the event description; if the invite has none, insert numbered empty slots and note "no agenda on invite".
   - Working sections: Discussion (one subheading per agenda item), **Decisions**, **Action items** as a three-column structure (What / Who / By when) ready to fill.
   - For recurring-rolling docs: prepend today's dated section at the top so newest notes lead.
4. Resolve the destination folder (match the meeting to its project folder when obvious; say where and why).
5. Show the plan (name, folder, filled header, section list) and create after `apply`. Return the link.
6. Offer follow-ups without doing them: attach to the event, share with attendees - each gated separately since both notify or expose content.

## Output format

```
Notes doc plan - for: <event title>, <date/time>
Will create: "<doc name>" in <folder>
Pre-filled: 6 attendees (2 declined, excluded) · agenda: 4 items from invite
Sections: Attendees ✓ | Agenda ✓ | Discussion (4 subheads) | Decisions (empty) | Action items (What/Who/When table)

Say `apply` to create.
--- after apply ---
📝 <doc name> → <link>
Want it attached to the calendar event or shared with attendees? (Both notify people - say which.)
```

## Do

- Verify @google-calendar and @google-drive connectors and discover exact tool names first
- Pull attendees and agenda from the live event - never fabricate an agenda the invite doesn't have
- Check for an existing/attached notes doc before creating one
- Structure Action items as What/Who/When from the start; retrofitting structure mid-meeting never happens
- Use the meeting's date in the title, not creation time (matters when prepping the night before)

## Don't

- Don't share the doc or edit the calendar event without a separate, explicit yes
- Don't include declined attendees in the attendee list
- Don't create a second notes doc for a recurring series that keeps a rolling doc - extend it
- Don't pad the template with sections the user's team doesn't use; the default five are plenty
- Don't leave placeholder text like "[agenda here]" - empty numbered slots read cleaner in a live meeting

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-drive, @google-calendar) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)
```

# /gdocs-report-from-data

Write Google Doc report from data sources.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The numbers exist (a Sheet of results, survey exports, a metrics tab) but the stakeholders need prose: a monthly status report, a post-campaign wrap-up, a findings write-up. This skill reads the data sources in Drive and drafts a narrative Google Doc where every figure traces back to a cell, not to imagination.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Data sources: which Sheet(s)/tab(s)/docs in Drive, and which ranges matter
- Audience and stakes: exec readout (short, conclusions first) vs team report (methods and detail) vs client-facing (polished, no internal jargon)
- The question the report must answer ("did the campaign work?", "where did Q2 money go?") - a report without a question becomes a data dump
- Comparison baseline if any: vs last period, vs target, vs plan; and target length

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Workspace-native formatting. Return Doc/Sheet/Slide links.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-drive** - Google Drive
  - MCP server key: `google-drive`
  - Example tools: `google-drive__search_files`, `google-drive__read_file`, `google-drive__create_file`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Reading data is read-only; **creating or updating the Doc is a write** - show the outline and key numbers first, create only after `apply`.
- Never overwrite an existing report doc; update by adding a new dated section or create a new doc, per the user's choice.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Drive tools, then read every named source. For Sheets, read the actual ranges - headers, units, and the totals row - and note data freshness (last modified). Flag up front if a source is stale, empty, or contradicts another source; do not paper over it.
2. Extract the numbers that answer the report's question: current values, the comparison baseline, and computed deltas (absolute and %). Keep a private figure ledger - every number destined for prose mapped to its source cell/range - and double-check arithmetic on any derived figure.
3. Decide the storyline **from the data**: lead with the finding ("spend held flat while signups rose 18%"), not with the existence of data ("we collected data on spend and signups"). Choose the 2-4 findings that matter to this audience; everything else goes to an appendix table.
4. Draft the Doc, structured for the audience:
   - **Exec**: TL;DR (3 bullets) → key numbers table → what we're doing about it. One page.
   - **Team**: Summary → findings with supporting tables → method/caveats → next steps.
   - **Client**: Context → results vs promised targets → recommendations.
   Use Doc-native formatting: Heading styles (so the outline pane works), real tables for figures, and a "Data sources" footer naming each Sheet with link and as-of date.
5. Mark judgment calls visibly: anything that is interpretation rather than measurement gets "our read:" phrasing; data gaps get an explicit "not measured" rather than a smoothed-over sentence.
6. Present the outline + the figure ledger's headline numbers for review; on `apply`, create the Doc in the agreed folder and return the link.

## Output format

```
Report plan - "<title>" for <audience>, answering: <the question>
Sources read: <Sheet/tab (as-of date)> ...  ⚠ <staleness/contradiction flags>

Proposed outline:
1. TL;DR - <the 3 bullets, drafted>
2. Key numbers - <table preview: metric | current | baseline | Δ>
3. <finding sections>
n. Data sources & caveats

Headline figures (each traced to a cell):
- Signups 4,812 (Sheet "Funnel"!C14) vs 4,077 last period → +18%
Say `apply` to create the Doc in <folder>.
--- after apply ---
📄 <title> → <link> · figures traceable via the Data sources footer
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-drive connector and discover exact tool names first
- Trace every number in the prose to a source cell; the figure ledger is the report's integrity
- Lead with findings; a report is an answer, not a tour of the spreadsheet
- State each source's as-of date - a June report built on April data must say so
- Recompute derived percentages yourself rather than trusting a possibly-broken sheet formula blindly; flag mismatches

## Don't

- Don't invent, extrapolate, or "smooth" numbers the sources don't contain - gaps are reported as gaps
- Don't bury the answer under methodology; caveats go after findings, not before
- Don't paste giant raw tables into the narrative - summarize in the body, full table in the appendix
- Don't write over an existing report doc without explicit instruction
- Don't mix measurement and interpretation in the same sentence without labeling which is which

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-drive) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)
```

5. Append as new rows at the true end of the data (above any totals row), matching column order exactly - never overwrite, never insert mid-table.
6. Verify by re-reading the appended range, then report the batch total, the new month-to-date figure if the sheet computes one, and any category that jumped notably.

## When to use

The team dashboard is a KPI tab in a Google Sheet, refreshed every Monday from raw-data tabs or figures the user brings. This skill computes the week's KPI values, writes them into the right period column/row, and reports movement against target - trend-aware, not just this week's snapshot.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Which Sheet, which KPI tab, and where source data lives (raw-data tabs in the same Sheet, another Sheet, or values supplied in chat)

- Targets: where they live in the sheet, and the red/amber/green convention if statuses are tracked

```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

Discover the Sheets/Drive tools, then read the KPI tab's anatomy: KPI rows and definitions, period columns and the sheet's period-label format ("W27" / "Jul 1-7"), target cells, and which cells contain formulas (some KPI tabs compute from raw tabs via formulas - those cells must not be overwritten with static numbers; the refresh may just mean extending formulas to a new column).

4. Build the new period entry: correct next column/row with a properly formatted period label, values in the sheet's units and precision, statuses per the sheet's RAG convention where used. If the tab extends formulas per period, propose the formula fill rather than static values.
5. Show the review table - every cell write, plus WoW/MoM delta and vs-target per KPI. Apply after `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`; re-read after writing to confirm.
6. Deliver the movement summary: streaks ("4th week above target"), crossings ("churn crossed target the wrong way"), and anything marked partial or anomalous.

## Output format

```
KPI refresh - <Sheet/tab>, writing period <label> into column <X>
Sources: <raw tab(s)> (complete ✅ / ⚠ data ends Wed - partial)

|---|-----|------|-----|---|--------|--------|----------|

Held back pending verification: #3 (value outside historical range - check raw tab)
Say `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,2`.

Written & verified. Movement notes: signups 4th straight week over target · churn crossed threshold - first time since Mar.

- Keep the sheet's own period-label format and units exactly

- Don't invent a KPI definition when the sheet or user hasn't given one

# /gslides-pitch-from-doc

Create Slides deck structure from Doc outline.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The thinking already lives in a Google Doc - a strategy memo, product one-pager, or proposal - and now it must be pitched. This skill converts the Doc's argument into a slide-by-slide deck plan (and builds the deck where tools allow): one idea per slide, headlines that assert rather than label, prose demoted to speaker notes.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Source Doc (link or name) and which sections are in/out of scope
- The pitch context: audience (investors, exec sponsor, customer), decision being sought, and time slot (10 minutes ≈ 8-10 slides; adjust)
- Where the deck should live: Google Slides in a Drive folder, or a local .pptx built with the `/pptx` skill
- Template/brand constraints, if a house deck template exists to copy

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Workspace-native formatting. Return Doc/Sheet/Slide links.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).

- **@powerpoint** - Microsoft Powerpoint (attach in prompt or enable Office MCP)
  - Local edits: run bundled skill `/pptx`

```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not give legal, medical, tax, investment, hiring, or security advice as a final authority.
- For sends, publishing, deletes, refunds, account changes, or other irreversible actions, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Drive tools and read the full source Doc - headings, body, tables, and any embedded charts. Extract the argument's skeleton: the problem, the proposal, the evidence, the ask.

Default arc for the slide count available: Title → The problem (stakes) → The proposal (one sentence) → Why it works (2-3 evidence slides, one point each) → Cost/plan → Risks & mitigations → The ask. | 3 | We can halve it with guided setup | 1 diagram placeholder | §Proposal |
| 4 | Pilot cut tickets 40% | chart candidate: Doc table 2 | §Results |
| ... |
| n | The ask: €25k + 1 engineer for Q3 | 2 bullets | §Budget |

Say `apply` to build in <Google Slides folder / local .pptx>.
--- after apply ---
🎞 <deck link/path> · speaker notes filled from Doc prose
Still needs a human: charts for slides 4, 6 · template colors · rehearse slide n's ask.

- Push the Doc's prose into speaker notes instead of onto slides

- Don't invent numbers, logos, or testimonials that aren't in the source Doc

# /gslides-quarterly-review

Fill quarterly review slide placeholders.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Every quarter the same review deck gets rebuilt by hand: same template, new numbers. This skill takes the team's QBR template (last quarter's deck or a placeholder template), pulls the quarter's metrics from the tracking Sheet, fills every placeholder, and drafts speaker notes - turning a day of deck-fiddling into a review-and-approve pass.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- The template: a placeholder deck ({{revenue}}, {{q_name}}-style), or last quarter's deck to clone-and-refill
- The quarter being reported and the metrics source(s): which Sheet/tab holds the quarter's actuals, targets, and prior-quarter values
- Narrative inputs numbers can't supply: top 3 wins, misses with reasons, next-quarter priorities - from the user or a named notes Doc
- Audience (leadership vs team vs board) - it sets how bluntly misses are framed in the notes

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Workspace-native formatting. Return Doc/Sheet/Slide links.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@powerpoint** - Microsoft Powerpoint (attach in prompt or enable Office MCP)
  - Local edits: run bundled skill `/pptx`

- **@google-drive** - Google Drive
  - MCP server key: `google-drive`
  - Example tools: `google-drive__search_files`, `google-drive__read_file`, `google-drive__create_file`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Never edit the template or last quarter's deck in place - always **copy first**, fill the copy.
- Show the filled-values table before writing into the copy; wait for `apply`.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Drive/Slides tools. Open the template deck read-only and inventory every placeholder: explicit tokens ({{revenue}}) or, when cloning last quarter's deck, every spot carrying old quarter-specific content - numbers, quarter labels ("Q1" in titles and footers), date stamps, old wins/misses text. The label-in-the-title is the classic one that slips through.
2. Pull the quarter's metrics from the source Sheet: actuals, targets, prior quarter (and year-ago where the deck compares YoY). Compute deltas and attainment %. Any metric missing from the source goes on a "needs input" list - never fill a placeholder with a guess.
3. Collect the narrative inputs (wins, misses + reasons, next-quarter priorities). Match each to its slide; where the user gave none, leave the placeholder visibly marked `[NEEDS INPUT: top win #2]` rather than inventing achievements.
4. Build the fill map - placeholder → new value → source - and present it as the review table, including every quarter-label swap. Charts bound to sheets are noted for refresh; static chart images are flagged for manual replacement (don't fake a chart).
5. On `apply`: copy the template to "<Team> QBR <quarter>", apply the fill map, and draft speaker notes per slide - what the number is, why it moved, what we're doing - with misses framed per audience (factual + owned, never hidden).
6. Verify by re-reading the copy for leftover placeholders/old-quarter strings, then deliver the link plus the punch list of `[NEEDS INPUT]` spots.

## Output format

```
QBR fill - template: "<deck>" → new copy "<Team> QBR <Qx FY26>"
Placeholders found: <n> (incl. 4 quarter-labels in titles/footers)

FILL MAP
| # | Slide | Placeholder | New value | Source |
|---|-------|------------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | 3 "Revenue" | {{revenue}} | €412k (target €400k, 103%) | Sheet "FY26"!D8 |
| 2 | 3 title | "Q1" → "Q2" | - | label swap |
| 3 | 7 "Wins" | win #2 | [NEEDS INPUT] | user |

Charts: slide 5 linked-chart will refresh · slide 8 is a static image - replace manually.
Say `apply` to copy the template and fill.
--- after apply ---
🎞 <new deck link> · leftover-placeholder scan: clean ✅ (except 1 marked NEEDS INPUT)
Speaker notes drafted on all metric slides. Punch list: win #2 text, slide 8 chart image.
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-drive connector (and `/pptx` or @powerpoint when the template is a .pptx) and discover exact tool names first
- Hunt quarter labels and dates everywhere - titles, footers, chart captions - not just the obvious number boxes
- Trace every filled number to its Sheet cell in the fill map
- Leave visible [NEEDS INPUT] markers instead of plausible-sounding filler
- Run the leftover-placeholder scan after filling; one surviving "{{...}}" in a board deck is the failure everyone remembers

## Don't

- Don't modify the template or prior deck - copy, then fill the copy
- Don't invent wins, reasons for misses, or forward-looking commitments
- Don't silently keep last quarter's number when the source lacks a new one - that's the worst failure mode of clone-and-refill
- Don't fabricate chart images; flag static charts for humans
- Don't spin misses in speaker notes beyond the audience-appropriate framing the user approved

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-drive, @powerpoint) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)

## Schedule

Recommended cadence: **quarterly**

Example: `/loop 7d Run /gslides-quarterly-review`
```

# /gworkspace-brd-writer

Write BRD in Google Docs from brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A project needs a Business Requirements Document and all that exists is a brief - a paragraph from a stakeholder, a kickoff-notes doc, an email thread's worth of intent. This skill expands that brief into a structured BRD in Google Docs: scope, requirements with IDs, success metrics, assumptions - with every gap in the brief flagged as an open question instead of silently invented.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- The brief: pasted text, or a Doc/notes file in Drive to read
- Project facts the brief usually omits: sponsor/owner, target users, hard deadline or budget ceiling, systems it must integrate with
- House format: an existing BRD in Drive to mirror section-for-section, or use the standard structure below
- Depth: lightweight one-pager BRD vs full document with traceable requirement IDs (REQ-001…)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Workspace-native formatting. Return Doc/Sheet/Slide links.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-drive** - Google Drive
  - MCP server key: `google-drive`
  - Example tools: `google-drive__search_files`, `google-drive__read_file`, `google-drive__create_file`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Creating the Doc is a write: show the section outline and the requirements table in chat first; create in Drive only after `apply`.
- A BRD makes commitments - clearly separate what the brief actually said from what you inferred, so stakeholders approve facts, not fabrications.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Drive tools; read the brief (and the house-format BRD if one was named - mirror its section order and heading names exactly).
2. Decompose the brief into the BRD's raw material, tagging each item **[stated]** (explicit in the brief), **[inferred]** (reasonable reading - must be verifiable by the sponsor), or **[gap]** (the brief is silent - becomes an open question, never filler prose).
3. Draft the BRD (standard structure when no house format):
   - **Executive summary** - the business problem and proposed outcome in 5 lines.
   - **Background & objectives** - why now; objectives phrased as measurable outcomes.
   - **Scope** - In and **Out** as separate lists; the Out-of-scope list prevents more project pain than any other section, so propose plausible exclusions and mark them [inferred] for confirmation.
   - **Stakeholders** - role, name, responsibility (RACI-lite).
   - **Requirements** - numbered REQ-001… rows: description, priority (MoSCoW), source (which line of the brief), acceptance criterion. Each requirement testable - "fast" becomes "page loads < 2s [inferred - confirm target]".
   - **Success metrics** - how the business will know it worked, with baseline if known.
   - **Assumptions & constraints** - every [inferred] item lands here or inline-tagged.
   - **Open questions** - every [gap], each assigned to the person who can answer it.
4. Quality pass: no requirement without an acceptance criterion; no objective without a metric; no orphan requirement that serves no stated objective (flag or cut).
5. Show outline + requirements table + open-questions list in chat for approval. On `apply`, create the Doc in the agreed folder using proper Heading styles (outline pane navigable) and a real table for requirements; return the link.
6. Offer next steps without doing them: share with the sponsor for comment, or spin the open questions into an email draft - each gated separately.

## Output format

```
BRD draft - "<project name>" from <brief source> · depth: <one-pager/full>

Outline: <the section list, one line>
Requirements extracted: <n> ([stated] <s> · [inferred] <i> · gaps → <g> open questions)

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Source | Acceptance criterion |
|----|-------------|----------|--------|----------------------|
| REQ-001 | ... | Must | brief §2 | ... |
| REQ-004 | ... | Should | [inferred] | ... - confirm with sponsor |

Open questions (blocking approval):
1. <question> → <who answers>
Say `apply` to create the Doc in <folder>.
--- after apply ---
📄 <BRD title> → <link>
Suggested next: share with <sponsor> for comments? (needs your go-ahead - sharing notifies)
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-drive connector and discover exact tool names first
- Tag stated vs inferred vs gap relentlessly - the BRD's value is knowing which is which
- Write every requirement with an ID, a priority, and a testable acceptance criterion
- Give Out-of-scope a real list, not "TBD"
- Mirror the house template's sections when one exists rather than imposing the standard structure

## Don't

- Don't pad gaps with plausible boilerplate - a wrong requirement in an approved BRD costs a project phase
- Don't write untestable requirements ("intuitive", "robust") without a confirmable criterion attached
- Don't merge assumptions into requirements; they live apart precisely so they get challenged
- Don't share the Doc or email stakeholders without separate explicit approval
- Don't present this as a signed-off spec - it's a draft for sponsor review, and the open-questions section says so

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-drive) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /gworkspace-brd-writer or describe the task from the skill description.
